name: Poems: "Teamwork" & "Courage" — both by Edgar Albert Guest

sourceRefs.label: 2024_q page 66-67 Q104-109 (real exam lines from both poems, with answer key)
civilserviceaspirants.in - dedicated MCQ sets for both poems (figure-of-speech per line)

researchNotes: Paired because both are by the same poet with a similar inspirational/moralizing short-verse style — genuinely useful pairing since author-bio facts apply to both at once, unlike the Crocodile/Railway Carriage pairing which was purely for length. Found real confirmed hits in the 2024 question bank: Q106 confirms 'It's written in victory and defeat, And every trial a man may meet' is from Courage (the word 'it' = Courage), and Q109 confirms 'It's helping your fellowman to score, When his chances hopeless seem' is from Teamwork (the word 'it' = Teamwork) — both with visible answer-key marks. The same page cluster (Q110-117) turned up bonus real confirmed lines for FOUR other syllabus poems not yet authored (Stick-Together Families, Life, Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening) plus one prose piece (The Tempest/Miranda) — saved separately to tools/literature-evidence-stash.md rather than lost, for reuse when those topics get authored. One important correction found in the same cluster: a 2024 question attributed 'The River' to 'Caroline Anne Bowles', but OUR syllabus's 'The River' is by Ilan Shamir — these are two different poems sharing a title; flagged so that mismatched old-paper trivia doesn't get mistakenly applied to our syllabus's actual poem when that topic is authored. The line-by-line figure-of-speech labels from the coaching site are presented as the exam's expected answers even where a few read as debatable on close literary reading (e.g. calling certain plain statements 'metaphor' or 'personification') — flagged once here rather than re-litigated per question, since matching the exam's expected answer matters more than winning a literary-criticism argument.

reading.intro: Both poems are short, didactic verse by the same American poet (Edgar Albert Guest, also responsible for 'The Stick-Together Families' elsewhere in this syllabus). Real exam evidence shows TNPSC tests these almost entirely via 'identify the figure of speech in this exact line' and 'what does the pronoun it/this refer to' — so the Q&A below drills both formats directly.

qa.question: What is the central message of "Courage"?
qa.answer: The poem defines courage by first saying what it ISN'T (not a brilliant dash, not instantaneous, not born of despair, not a creature of flickered hope), then says what it IS: something deep in the soul of man, always working to serve a plan, present in both victory and defeat, and in every trial a person faces. This 'define by negation, then affirmation' structure is itself worth recognizing for appreciation-style questions.

qa.question: Real exam line: "It's written in victory and defeat, And every trial a man may meet." What does 'it' refer to?
qa.answer: Courage — this exact line, with this exact pronoun-reference question, appeared in a real TNPSC paper (2024).

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "Courage isn't a brilliant dash" and "But it's something deep in the soul of man."
qa.answer: Both lines are cited as Metaphor — courage is described AS a 'dash' / AS something physically 'deep' inside a person, without using 'like/as'.

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "Born of despair with a sudden spring" and "It isn't a creature of flickered hope" and "That is working always to serve some plan."
qa.answer: All three are cited as Personification — courage (an abstract idea) is given creature-like or human-like qualities (being 'born', being a 'creature', 'working' toward a 'plan').

qa.question: What is the central message of "Teamwork"?
qa.answer: Individual fame and being 'singled out' for glory matter less than contributing to the team — 'there's no lone hand in the game we play.' True worth is shown by how you 'pull with the team' and work with your 'running mate' toward a 'bigger scheme', not by solo achievement.

qa.question: Real exam line: "It's helping your fellowman to score, When his chances hopeless seem." What does 'it' refer to?
qa.answer: Teamwork — this exact line, with this exact pronoun-reference question, appeared in a real TNPSC paper (2024).

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "And it's fine to be counted a star" and "For there's no lone hand in the game we play" and "We must work to a bigger scheme."
qa.answer: All three are cited as Metaphor — being 'a star', having a 'lone hand' (a card-game image for going it alone), and a 'bigger scheme' are all non-literal stand-ins for the poem's real subject (individual glory vs. teamwork).

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "It's all very well to have courage and skill" and "And the thing that counts in the world to-day."
qa.answer: Both lines are cited as Alliteration in the source material.

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "How do you pull with the team?"
qa.answer: Synecdoche — 'pull' (a single physical action, as in rowing or tug-of-war) stands in for a person's entire contribution and effort within the team.

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "They may single you out for fame."
qa.answer: Irony — cited as ironic given the poem's overall argument that individual fame/recognition is less meaningful than team contribution; being 'singled out' runs against the poem's central value.

questions.prompt: "It's written in victory and defeat, And every trial a man may meet." The word 'it' in this real exam line refers to:
"It's helping your fellowman to score, When his chances hopeless seem." The word 'it' in this real exam line refers to:
Which figure of speech is used in "Courage isn't a brilliant dash"?
Which figure of speech is used in "Born of despair with a sudden spring"?
questions.explanation: Personification — courage is described as being 'born'.

questions.prompt: Which figure of speech is used in "It isn't a creature of flickered hope"?
Which figure of speech is used in "For there's no lone hand in the game we play" (from Teamwork)?
questions.explanation: Metaphor — 'lone hand' (a card-game image) stands for going it alone.

questions.prompt: Which figure of speech is used in "How do you pull with the team?"
questions.explanation: Synecdoche — 'pull' represents the whole of a person's team contribution.

questions.prompt: Which figure of speech is used in "They may single you out for fame"?
questions.explanation: Irony — being singled out for individual fame runs against the poem's team-first message.

questions.prompt: "And it's fine to be counted a star" uses which figure of speech?
questions.explanation: Metaphor — 'a star' stands for being exceptionally accomplished.
Edgar Albert Guest wrote both — and also 'The Stick-Together Families' elsewhere in this syllabus.
